title: Frolicking With AI Generated Art
text:
We are living in the times where all art forms that were solely reserved for humans is slowly being generated by AI. Be it the art of writing, music, images, video creation, and even pure art!
2022 began wonderfully with the advent of AI systems that could create art with mere textual descriptions. Platforms like DALL.E.2, Mindjourney, and many more have emerged gaining high popularity across the world.
I was a little away from the AI buzz news, and I got to know about it a little later when I was listening to Lesson 1 of the Practical Deep Learning for Coders course offered by fast.ai. Jeremy Howard, the instructor of the course shared these amazing explorations done by people who toyed with this new state of the art AI beauty!
I had to go and explore them myself. I just had to! I mean, just look at these.
So, I signed up of DALL.E.2 and started to experiment with it. In this blog I am sharing some of my explorations which were mind bending.

title: Frolicking With AI Generated Art
text:
A Challenge I Faced
I had this wonderful machine infront of me, that was waiting for my textual instructions to create art! The first challenge I faced was the inability to express what I wanted to see. I mean, I am a human after all, and I must be really good with the primary skills that I possess as a human, to articulate myself. But, there I was staring blankly at the search bar, thinking what to type. All I could come up with was “fox eating an ice cream”, which is a good thing to begin with. It did give me results of “a fox eating an ice cream”.



DALL.E.2 Illustration


But I couldn’t articulate longer descriptive sentences. I spent some time thinking and grabbing ideas from other peoples search styles and finally I was able to describe something expressive and detailed. Let me show you its wonders.

title: Frolicking With AI Generated Art
text:
My DALL.E.2 Explorations

abstract portrait of a man made with flowers
























abstract art of a robot teaching math to kids
























scholastic deep explorations in snow
























3d render of a book made with melting cheese placed on dining table
























a flower riding a horse in space meeting aliens



















a dog wearing glasses teaching robots in a class
























samurai fighting a battle with a katana on fire























Isn’t it amazing?! Feel free to try yourself too!

title: Frolicking With AI Generated Art
text:
A Minor Epiphany
I might be stepping on a slightly contreversial theme here, but on a bit of a different philosophical tangent, I had a minor epiphany that I want to share.
As I was struggling to articulate descriptions of art I wanted see, it crossed my mind that although we are moving towards this amazing world of possibilities where AI can do brilliantly magical stuff, but we as humans are also losing parts of ourselves which were innate to our cognitive abilities. We are in a sense delegating our cognitive tools to AI and dumbing ourselves down. I mean what will be left of us when every possible capability of our minds is transfered to AI. There were times, when we would smirk at the concept of doing mental arithmetics thinking that our mobile phones can do it for us, which they can for sure, very accurately. But we are losing our mental abilities to machines to a point that we can not even use it the way we want.
To end on a lighter note, here are a few art to cheer you up!


fox walking down the street to a bar on a rainy night holding an umbrella

title: Oh! Segmentation Tasks, Segmentation Tasks
text:
Street view image created using Stable Diffusion


Can you recognize various objects from the above picture? Our eyes have the ability to see a complete image and recognize the distinguishing objects from it. We know there is a car on the left, a person ahead on the right, etc. Can we make computers do this?! Let us find out!

title: Oh! Segmentation Tasks, Segmentation Tasks
text:
Street view image created using Stable Diffusion


Can you recognize various objects from the above picture? Our eyes have the ability to see a complete image and recognize the distinguishing objects from it. We know there is a car on the left, a person ahead on the right, etc. Can we make computers do this?! Let us find out!

title: Oh! Segmentation Tasks, Segmentation Tasks
text:
What Is Segmentation?
Segmentation refers to the process of dividing an image into multiple regions or segments, where each segment corresponds to a specific object or background.
The goal of a segmentation model in Deep Learning is to recognize the content of every individual pixel in an image thereby localizing objects in it.
This task is very important for self-driving cars, for example. If a self-driving car doesn’t know where a pedestrian is, then it doesn’t know how to avoid one!

title: Oh! Segmentation Tasks, Segmentation Tasks
text:
Building A Segmentation Model
We will train a simple segmentation model using the fastai library.



Source: https://giphy.com/gifs/starwars-star-wars-the-last-jedi-xT9Iguc1FSPtLmCw5W



Installing & Importing Relevant Libraries
We will install the fastai software and export all the vision libraries, since this is computer vision task.

!pip install -Uqqq fastai


from fastai.vision.all import *



Getting Training Data
We will use a subset of the CamVid dataset from the paper “Semantic Object Classes in Video: A High-Definition Ground Truth Database” by Gabriel J. Brostow et al.
The following code gets the data for us!

path = untar_data(URLs.CAMVID_TINY)
path






    
      
      100.18% [2318336/2314212 00:00<00:00]
    
    


Path('/root/.fastai/data/camvid_tiny')


Now that we got the data, before using it for training our model, we we will have to specify fastai what kind of data we have, how it is structured, and for what task are we going to use it.
The following code does this.

dls = SegmentationDataLoaders.from_label_func(path, bs = 8, fnames = get_image_files(path/'images'),
                                             label_func = lambda o: path/'labels'/f'{o.stem}_P{o.suffix}',
                                             codes = np.loadtxt(path/'codes.txt', dtype = str))

Let us have a look at our data!

dls.show_batch()








These are the 8 images for which we will train a model that can recognize its objects.


Using A Learner & Fine-tuning
We will use a unet learner and resenet34 architecture, a pre-trained model to fine-tune it for our task of segmentation.

learner = unet_learner(dls, resnet34)
learner.fine_tune(10)

/usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages/torchvision/models/_utils.py:208: UserWarning: The parameter 'pretrained' is deprecated since 0.13 and may be removed in the future, please use 'weights' instead.
  warnings.warn(
/usr/local/lib/python3.10/dist-packages/torchvision/models/_utils.py:223: UserWarning: Arguments other than a weight enum or `None` for 'weights' are deprecated since 0.13 and may be removed in the future. The current behavior is equivalent to passing `weights=ResNet34_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1`. You can also use `weights=ResNet34_Weights.DEFAULT` to get the most up-to-date weights.
  warnings.warn(msg)
Downloading: "https://download.pytorch.org/models/resnet34-b627a593.pth" to /root/.cache/torch/hub/checkpoints/resnet34-b627a593.pth
100%|██████████| 83.3M/83.3M [00:00<00:00, 298MB/s]

The training is done! It trained for about 15-20 sec. 
Let us see the results!

learner.show_results(figsize = (7, 8))















Voila! On the left are the original images, and on the right are the ones that were segmented by our model. We can clearly see that it did a pretty good job in recognizing the pixels! For some, it got a few things wrong, but for most of them it got right!
To summarize, computers now can identify objects in an image, that too pretty well!

title: Oh! Segmentation Tasks, Segmentation Tasks
text:
A Poem
Here is a poem generated by ChatGPT on Segmentation.

Oh, segmentation task, segmentation task, Dividing images is its awesome task. To split an image into regions fine, And label each one, that is the line.
Segmentation finds objects in a snap, And separates them from the background’s trap. In autonomous cars it plays a role, To avoid obstacles and reach the goal.
In medical images it helps a lot, To pinpoint where the issues are wrought. Convolutional neural networks take charge, And extract features like a boss, it’s large!
They label each pixel with a keen eye, And categorize them, oh so sly. Object recognition, it can aid, And much more, the list won’t fade.
Segmentation task, oh how grand, In computer vision, it’s in high demand! Precise and accurate, it must be, So many applications, it sets us free!

title: What Can We Blog About
text:
Blogging is highly encouraged and advised especially if we are in tech. If we decide to start a blog, the question that comes naturally is What should we blog about?.
In search of the answers for this specific question, I did a bit of research.
This post lays out a summary of the research I did and my understanding of some of the various things that we can blog about.
There are more things we can blog about than the ones listed here. But I picked the ones that seemed more relevant and serve as a general use case.
In total, there are five things mentioned here.
They are as follows:
For each of these above items, I layout the following:
At the end, I share a few resources.

title: What Can We Blog About
text:
The Feynman Technique
This is essentially, attempting to teach whatever we have learned through our post by writing about it.

Pros

Helps us understand, organize, and synthesis our knowledge for us
Reveals gaps in our understanding
Provides our own unique perspective of the topic for the learners



Cons

Time Consuming
This process takes a lot of time. We could just think in our minds and attempt to teach it by speaking out loud or just thinking without having to write. If our thinking skills are sharp, that will be enough. Also, when we learn something new, instead of spending time on writing a blog post about it, we could move on and learn newer things for ourselves.

Overcoming Tips

Instead of writing a blog post of everything that we learn, we can choose to write a post on topics that are considered hard or not so easy to grasp. This way, we end up writing fewer posts but filled with quality.




Risk of being wrong and not qualified
There are high chances that the things, we attempt to teach will have flaws in it. We might be wrong on some matters or our understanding might not be accurate. We are learners at the end of the day and not qualified in any form, yet!

Overcoming Tips

Don’t come from a place of being an expert on the topic.
Let the readers know that we have no authority or official expertise.
Let the readers know that we are just sharing our own understanding of the topic.
Let them know that we can be wrong.

title: What Can We Blog About
text:
Sharing our learning/coding journey
Here, we essentially maintain a log of our learning/coding journey. The ups and downs, the gotcha moments, and so on.

Pros

Good self-reflection practice
Helps learners who are a few steps behind us. I myself have personally learned a lot from other people’s experiences and journeys.



Cons

Susceptible to vulnerability.
Well not exactly a cons depending on how we look it. Some of us might think that being vulnerable and sharing our journeys is a sign of courage and maturity. Our vulnerable moments on the wrong hands might be something to think about. People who have a good heart and are wise will understand all this and not ridicule us.

Overcoming Tips

I am not going to step hard on this as it is a matter of personal choice.

Currently, the way I look at this is as follows: We must be able to showcase our learning weaknesses and our vulnerable side to the world. Everyone sucks when they begin. All the great people sucked so damn badly. Being not good enough is not something to be ashamed of. What do we do next?! How do we improve?! That is what matters.

title: What Can We Blog About
text:
Sharing great resources
Along our learning journeys, we discover many new resources. Sometimes underrated ones that are hidden gems. We think, “Gosh! Why do people not know about this?!”. We can curate a list of such resources and share it in our blog post.

Pros

A record for ourselves, if we need it. However, I personally, was advised by my mentor to not rely on it and start from fresh and research again to discover even newer and amazing resources.
Helping the resource creators’s work reach out to more people. They will appreciate it!



Cons

Subjective Nature

The resources, we found amazing might not be amazing to our readers. Everyone has their own taste.


Overcoming Tips
Again we don’t enforce this list to anyone, we just let our readers know that “Hey! I found these resources helpful when I was learning. Maybe you can try them out for yourself.”

title: What Can We Blog About
text:
Creating summaries
This is essentially creating short summaries of long form content (both offline as well as online) such as the following:

Videos
Books
Conferences
Talks
Classes


Pros

Capturing the essence of an source
Ensuring strong grasp over what was said.



Cons

Note-taking trap
We might fall into the trap of writing lengthy notes.

Overcoming Tips

Keep it short and simple
Provide our perspectives on the source

title: What Can We Blog About
text:
Most common How-tos
As a coder, there are many things that we do regularly almost everyday as part of our workflow. They barely change. They are fixed routine tasks. Like, setting up our JavaScript projects, setting up a cloud database, etc. It would be better to have them listed and simplified at a place instead of going back to the resources and figuring out every time.

Pros

Serves as a quick cheat sheet



Cons

Subjective Nature
These will be our workflows and might not be applicable to our readers.

Overcoming Tips

Explain a brief reasoning behind the steps. If our readers align with our philosophy and background, they can adopt it.

title: What Can We Blog About
text:
My Summary
Based on my current scenario, I have decided to blog about the following three things:

Sharing my learning/coding journey
Most common How-tos
Sharing great resources

title: What Can We Blog About
text:
Related Posts
Following are some of the related posts, that I plan to write later. They however, serve as good questions to ask ourselves and explore.

Why should we blog?
Why shouldn’t we blog?
What not to blog about?
How to blog?

title: What Can We Blog About
text:
Resources

A Complete Step-by-Step Guide to Start a Blog for Software Developers - The Ankur Tyagi
Why you (yes, you) should blog. The top advice I would give my younger… | by Rachel Thomas | Medium
Advice for Better Blog Posts – fast.ai
How to Blog to Advance Your Career and Learn Faster - YouTube
“advice” for aspiring tech bloggers

title: How To Run A Linux Subsystem In Windows
text:
One of the many first steps when venturing into doing Data Science is to have access to a Unix/Linux/Ubuntu OS.
But having a Windows OS and wanting to setup and run this hardcore geeky OS might be challenging.
In this blog, I will show a very quick and a simple way of setting up a Linux Subsystem in your Windows OS that I learned from Jeremy Howard! If you wish to follow along, you will have a complete full fledge Linux OS running in your Windows in the next 3-5 minutes. No complicated hacking stuff, no dual booting, no splitting of your computer into two halves using a light beam, none of that stuff. And if you are wondering if you will lose all the Windows features, don’t worry, you won’t.
Let us get started!



Source: https://giphy.com/gifs/wikitude-augmented-reality-penguin-linux-4Zgy9QqzWU8C3ugvCa

title: How To Run A Linux Subsystem In Windows
text:
One of the many first steps when venturing into doing Data Science is to have access to a Unix/Linux/Ubuntu OS.
But having a Windows OS and wanting to setup and run this hardcore geeky OS might be challenging.
In this blog, I will show a very quick and a simple way of setting up a Linux Subsystem in your Windows OS that I learned from Jeremy Howard! If you wish to follow along, you will have a complete full fledge Linux OS running in your Windows in the next 3-5 minutes. No complicated hacking stuff, no dual booting, no splitting of your computer into two halves using a light beam, none of that stuff. And if you are wondering if you will lose all the Windows features, don’t worry, you won’t.
Let us get started!



Source: https://giphy.com/gifs/wikitude-augmented-reality-penguin-linux-4Zgy9QqzWU8C3ugvCa

title: How To Run A Linux Subsystem In Windows
text:
Step 0: Getting A Terminal
We will need a terminal handy right after we are done setting up Linux! There are many options to choose from. Let us download and install the Windows Terminal from the Microsoft Store before proceeding.

Press the “⊞” key and type “store”.



Microsoft Store search


Open the Microsoft Store and search for “windows terminal”.



Download & install Windows Terminal


Download, and install it. We will use this soon!

title: How To Run A Linux Subsystem In Windows
text:
Step 1: Getting the Linux Subsystem
We will be setting up our subsystem from the Windows Powershell application.

Press the “⊞” key button and search for “powershell”.



Powershell search


Click on Run as Administrator. It will prompt to verify. Click Yes.
Once you are inside the Windows Powershell, type the wsl --install command and press “⏎”.



wsl-command


This will download and install 2 things, the Windows Subsystem for Linux and Ubuntu as shown below. To take effect we need to reboot our system.
After the installation is done, close all the files and applications, and restart the computer.

title: How To Run A Linux Subsystem In Windows
text:
Step 2: Hello, Linux
Upon restarting your computer a powershell window will pop up automatically, welcoming you and launching the Linux Subsystem. Once it is launched, it will ask to set up a username and a password. This is like a new computer that is running!

Put a username and a password and you are done!



All set



What you have now is a full fledged Linux Subsystem running in your Windows.

title: How To Run A Linux Subsystem In Windows
text:
Step 3: Accessing Linux
Remember that Windows Terminal we kept ready at the beginning?! We will be using that to acsess our Linux CLI.

Open the Windows Terminal. By default, the Windows Powershell will run.



Powershell as default


There will be a little arrown pointing as shown. Click it, and will show the list of options we can select from. To get Linux, we will have to select manually from this list. Instead, let us setup Linux as the default profile whenever we open our Windows Terminal. To do that and go to Settings.



Settings


Make Linux or Ubuntu as the default one.



making Linux/Ubuntu as default



Next time you open the Windows Terminal, it will welcome you with Linux/Ubuntu.









Linux







Ubuntu

title: How To Run A Linux Subsystem In Windows
text:
Explore
You are all set to dwell into the Linux metaverse! If you are new to Linux, check out some tutorials, like this one to learn about it.

title: How This Course Will Work
text:
The title of the lesson is How This Course Will Work.
It is not clear what this is!
Is it the Foundations course, or the complete curriculum of The Odin Project?
However, from the title we can say that this lesson is about either (or both) of the following two things:
The lesson is divided into the following nine sections:

title: How This Course Will Work
text:
Introduction

The Odin Project is an open-source community dedicated to providing the best information sources to take you from zero to a full-stack developer.

The lesson begins by telling us the following two things:

What is The Odin Project?
What they do?


What is The Odin Project?
With regards to this, they say that:

The Odin Project is an open-source community…

Let us break this down.

community

a group of people.

open-source community

a community(a group of people) that is involved in the development of open source software.

open-source software

a software whose code us made publicly available.


I just got a sense of what this is. The following links were helpful:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_source
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open-source_software
https://opensource.com/resources/what-open-source
https://opensource.com/article/18/2/coining-term-open-source-software (Cool stuff)

In conclusion, The Odin Project is a group of people that are involved in the development of open-source software.

What open-source software does The Odin Project develop?
The above question arises naturally.
I think it is this curriculum itself.
I am not aware of any other “product” that they are working on.



What they do?
The next bit is around what they do. They are

…dedicated to providing the best information sources to take you from zero to a full-stack developer.


The journey from a zero to a Full Stack Developer
At the moment of starting this course, most of us are a zero at web development. From this zero, we would like to become a Full Stack Developer.

Many ways
In order to make this transition happen, there can be many ways:

Way 1
Way 2
…
Way n


One way: Learn from the best sources
One of the ways is learning from the best sources. Sources that already exist on the internet and else where.
However, to learn from the best sources, one must first get them. This involves a lot of work which I am not going to go into right now.
Folks at The Odin Project are dedicated to getting and providing these to us.
AZ: So the content in the curriculum is not completely their own.





What we will do in the Foundations course?

In this unit, we’ll learn about how the web works and start thinking about the basics of computer and web programming.

Next, they tell us what we will do in this unit.
Unit means course.
Here, when they say this unit, they are referring to the complete Foundations course.
The web has a working of its own.
In the Foundations course, we will do the following two things overally:

Learn about how the web works
Start thinking about the basics of computer and web programming

Basics does not mean simple.

basic

forming an essential foundation or starting point; fundamental.




The Foundations course covers baseline knowledge of web development

Each of the following sections and lessons covers essential baseline knowledge.


baseline knowledge

the minimum level of knowledge/understanding/skill that one must possess in a given setting.



How is this useful?

Even if you have no intention of becoming a web developer, this material should help you gain a useful understanding of the moving parts involved in creating and serving content on the web.

There are various moving parts involved in creating and serving content on the web.
Even if we have no intention of becoming a web developer, the stuff covered in the Foundations course should help us gain a useful understanding of these moving parts.



Learnings goals and plan

We will start by getting familiar with the internet and your own computer. Next, we’ll set up a development environment and learn about Git and GitHub. Then, we’ll go over the basics of front-end technologies like HTML, CSS, and JavaScript before stepping into the back end with a brief foray that covers the basic concepts of back-end technologies.

Below is an orderly list of things we will do in the Foundations course:

Get familiar with the internet
Get familiar with our own computer
Set up a development environment
Learn about Git
Learn about GitHub
Go over the basics of the following front-end technologies:

HTML
CSS
JS

Step into the back end with a brief raid that covers the basic concepts of back-end technologies.



Learning outcomes of the Foundations course

By the end of this unit, you should not only understand how the web works but also be able to identify and differentiate between all the technologies that you will use to build your own web applications. You will be able to build a basic webpage, style it, and add minor elements of interactivity while working comfortably from the command line.

First let us know the following three points:

The web has a working of its own (as mentioned earlier).
To build web applications various technologies are used.

interactivity

the ability of a computer to respond to a user’s input



Now, let us look at the learning outcomes.
By the end of this unit(Foundations course), we will:

Understand how the web works.
Be able to identify and differentiate between all the technologies that are used to build web applications.
Be able to build a basic webpage, style it, and add minor elements of interactivity while working comfortably from the command line.



Breadth of the Introduction section

This section intentionally covers a very broad range of topics.

This lesson is the first lesson of the Introduction section in the Foundations course.
They say that the Introduction section of the Foundations course covers a broad range of topics and this is intentional.
There are a total of five lessons in this section. Let us explore this breadth when we get there.

Why does the Introduction section cover a broad range of topics?

It’s silly to go diving straight into server-side programming without having a context for what it is and why it’s useful (and why you should learn it!).

We are going to dive into this thing called server-side programming after the Introduction section.
And before we dive into it we need a context for it.

Why do we need a context?
They say that it is silly to go diving straight into server-side programming without having a context for it.


What kind of context do we need?
We need to know at least the following three things about server-side programming:

What it is?
Why it’s useful?
Why we should learn it?



What is server-side programming?
Although this is going to be covered in the upcoming lessons, let us Google to get a simple idea.
When I Googled, there were lot of technicalities about this.
In simple terms server-side programming refers to backend development or programming on the server.
Its counter part is client-side programming which is frontend development.

title: How This Course Will Work
text:
Lesson overview

About this section

This section contains a general overview of topics that you will learn in this lesson.

These are essentially the learning outcomes of the lesson.


Learning outcomes
Here is a general overview of topics that we will learn in this lesson.

Understand how The Odin Project’s curriculum works.
Understand how the Foundations course works.


[!note] We have already explored much about how the Foundations course work. I feel that this section must have been put earlier.

title: How This Course Will Work
text:
How it works
By it here, they mean the The Odin Project’s curriculum.
This section is about “How The Odin Project’s curriculum works”.

This curriculum works by aggregating the best content from across the internet to teach a specific topic. In each lesson, we’ll introduce the topic and try to provide some useful context before pointing you to external resources made by others.

Consider a specific topic, let us say How the web works?.
There exists various content on the internet around that topic. They collect and gather the best of these.
Each topic is taught in a lesson.
In a lesson, they:

Introduce the topic to us
Try to provide some useful context
Point us to the external resources made by others.


Questions, Exercises, and Projects

Most lessons will contain questions that you should be able to answer before moving on. Some lessons will include exercises for you to complete. In addition, we provide several projects throughout the curriculum to help you grow your understanding by actually building things.

The questions in a lesson are like a checklist to tick-off and act like a measuring tool if we have completed the lesson.
If we can comfortably answer the questions, we can safely move to the next lesson.


What not to think of The Odin Project or programming?

Try not to think of The Odin Project, or programming, as a class in school. It’s not material you learn all at once to take a test, and then pass or fail.

They are associating The Odin Project with programming. When we are doing The Odin Project, we are essentially doing programming.
They say that we should not think of The Odin Project, or programming, as a class in school.
What happens in a class in school?
In a typical class in school, we learn some material all at once to take a test, and then pass or fail.
Programming is not like this - learning some material all at once!


What to think of The Odin Project or programming?

You can think of it as a snowball. You, yourself, are a snowball. You’re rolling down a hill full of snow; the further you roll, the more snow will stick to you. Sure, snow will also fall off you, and you’ll forget things often, but that’s just part of the process. Don’t worry if you reach a project and feel like you haven’t retained or memorized anything. That’s natural and happens to everyone. The projects are not tests of what you have memorized so far. They are practice points where the information will come back to you as you start solving your problems one at a time, relying on Google and the Odin Community for help.

I like this idea.
Turns out it is called as Snowball effect.
Also, they are not emphasizing on memory at all!

title: How This Course Will Work
text:
A note about tools

We often get questions about why this course does not cover a language or a tool.

The Odin Project does not cover a specific language or a tool.
They often get asked why this is the case.

Why?

The truth is that once you complete this curriculum, you will not need a course like this or any sort of “tutorial”.

This is so freaking cool! I love it! We will join those elite folks who don’t chase tutorials and courses!
In a way they are making us capable enough!

The Odin Project’s goal is not to teach you any standalone skills and it is not a tutorial.

Their reason is simply they don’t want to get involved in these petty stuff and it is not their goal.
The Odin Project’s goal is not to teach us any isolated standalone skills. This is what a tutorial does and The Odin Project is not a tutorial.


What then is the goal of The Odin Project?

The main idea is to teach you the why and change the way you think.

The Odin Project’s main goal is do the following two things:

Teach us the why of things?
Change the way we think.

I am not sure what they mean by change the way we think! I am hoping it is for the good!

One of the most difficult parts about this course is adopting the right mindset. An even bigger challenge is learning the core ideas of programming. It isn’t just about coding, it is about problem-solving, asking the right questions, and doing enough research. Getting into a position where you are comfortable with the skills will mean that you will also be comfortable at expanding your skill-set. Once you have a solid base, it will be simpler to expand upon it. This is exactly what The Odin Project is about: developing these skills.

There are various parts of this course.
Some of these parts are the most difficult ones.
One of these is adopting the right mindset.
AZ: What is the right mindset?
AZ: How do we adopt it?
An even bigger challenge is learning the core ideas of programming.
They say that programming is not just about coding. It is about the following four things:

Coding
Problem-solving
Asking the right questions
Doing enough research


After completing the course you will be in a position where you can simply jump straight into the documentation for anything you are trying to learn. Reading the documentation is the best way to learn any tooling. But it takes skill to master. Many people feel intimidated by the technical jargon, which is understandable. People who write documentations usually assume a background in programming. This is exactly why we give you plenty of material to read on your own.

The Odin Project makes us capable of learning a new language or a tool independently directly from the official documentation itself.

The skills you will gain from completing The Odin Project will be the foundation that you will be building upon for years and decades to come. If you come out of the course thinking that you need another course like this one to learn something like Python, then you either don’t believe in yourself or you haven’t taken away the important ideas that are covered in this course.


The skills you learn here are transferable to various contexts. Once you know the idea and that it is possible to do something, actually finding out how to do that thing becomes significantly easier and is simply a Google search away most of the times.

Imagine that we are in a new context. All that matters are the following things:

What to do? (The idea)
Is it possible to do it?
Finding out how to do it.

The last step is simply a Google search away most of the time. But figuring out the first two is what we must master.

title: How This Course Will Work
text:
A note on human language

From where does The Odin Project attracts people from?
The Odin Project attracts people from all over the world.


What kind of people join The Odin Project
People who aspire to learn how to become developers join The Odin Project.


Language of The Odin Project’s curriculum

Please be aware that this curriculum is written in English and maintained by English speakers.

The curriculum is written in English.

Translations
There are no translations.

Why not?
The maintainers are:

Not able to
Not expected to





Why English?

The programming world is rooted in the English language

As you develop into a programmer, you will find that the world you are entering into is firmly rooted in the English language. This means that the syntax of your programming language, the documentation that teaches you how to use it, and the majority of the people in the community are all expecting to communicate with you in English.




To the non-English speakers

If you are a non-English speaker, or English is not a primary language for you, this fact is not meant to discourage you, but to prepare you.

If some of us are any of the following two categories:

non-English speakers
English is not our primary language,

Then what has been said above is not meant to discourage us, but to prepare us.

Tips for preparation

As another part of this preparation, we highly recommend spending extra time on topics and terms that you do not understand right away. We also encourage you to seek out additional resources that teach about these topics in your own native language so you can understand them more fully.
In addition to this, you might also consider using a translation dictionary in your own language alongside our curriculum so you can readily reference it as you go.

They give the following three tips:

Spend extra time on topics and terms that are not understandable right away.
Seek out additional resources that teach about these topics in our own native language.
Use a translation dictionary in our own language alongside the curriculum so you can readily reference it as you go


Which translation dictionary to use?

We do not have any recommendations on these…

They do not have any recommendations on these.

Why not?

…since there are such a wide variety of languages spoken throughout the world, finding one may perhaps be a good first step in learning how to find useful tools online by searching. This is a skill you will utilize and improve as you progress in your learning to be a developer.


There are such a wide variety of languages spoken throughout the world.
Finding one may perhaps be a good first step in learning how to find useful tools online by searching. This is a skill you will utilize and improve as you progress in your learning to be a developer.

title: How This Course Will Work
text:
What comes next

How will we feel at the end of the Foundations course?

Once you’ve completed this course, you should feel comfortable with the building blocks of web programming but itching to dig deeper.

After completing the Foundations course we will:

Feel comfortable with the building blocks of web development.
Feel an itch to dig deeper



The Foundations course just gives a taste

Though we spend a fair bit of time digging into each of the major topics in this course, it’s really just a taste of what comes next (and all the cool stuff you can do with it).



Ok seriously! What next?!

The last lesson of this course will give you an opportunity to choose between a Full Stack JavaScript and Full Stack Rails path…

The last lesson of the Foundations course will give us an opportunity to choose between the following two paths:

Full Stack JavaScript path
Full Stack Rails path



About these paths

…, both designed to further develop the foundation established in this course. Each path will focus on taking these raw building blocks and honing them into a highly functional skillset.

Both these paths are designed to further develop the foundation established in the Foundations course.
Each path will focus on taking the raw building blocks equipped and honing them into a highly functional skillset.


The curriculum is of rich quality

The Odin Project is maintained by professionals. We have chosen some of the best resources available and curated a guide on how to go through them. If there are no good resources, we write our own.



Do not skip anything

With that said, know that everything in the curriculum is intentionally included and vital for you to become a successful programmer.


Why we shouldn’t skip anything?

As you move forward in the curriculum, each portion is built on everything that came before it…


Ya so?!

…, so skipping things will create pockets of non-understanding in your knowledge that start to affect your ability to solve problems and understand the task at hand.

Skipping things will create pockets of non-understanding in our knowledge.
These pockets will start to affect the following two things:

Our ability to solve problems
Our ability to understand the task at hand

Shit! This is scary!



Additional resources: The only section that we can skip

Additional resources are the only thing that is considered optional unless explicitly stated.

Forget skipping lessons, the only thing skippable are these small sections called Additional Resources in a lesson.
Sometimes even these are told to explicitly finish!

Purpose of the Additional Resources section

These are here in case you feel like you need or want to dive deeper into a topic to get a better understanding.

title: How This Course Will Work
text:
Assignment
There are two assignments.

Assignment 1

Learn more about The Odin Project by reading its about page.


Read The Odin Project’s about page.

Purpose: To learn more about The Odin Project.


Assignment 2

Check out the frequently asked questions section.


Check out the frequently asked questions section.

About

The following questions are an opportunity to reflect on key topics in this lesson. If you can’t answer a question, click on it to review the material, but keep in mind you are not expected to memorize or master this knowledge.

This is the “Questions” section they mentioned earlier.
In a lesson various topics are covered.
Some of these topics are key topics.
How is a topic classified as a key topic? They are doing it for us.
The questions mentioned in this section give us an opportunity to reflect on these key topics.
If we can not answer a question, we click on it to review the material.
We need to keep in mind that we are not expected to:

Memorize this knowledge
Master this knowledge

title: Overview
text: This is where it all begins! A hands-on introduction to all of the essential tools you’ll need to build real, working websites. You’ll learn what web developers actually do – the foundations you’ll need for later courses.

title: Overview
text: This is where it all begins! A hands-on introduction to all of the essential tools you’ll need to build real, working websites. You’ll learn what web developers actually do – the foundations you’ll need for later courses.

title: Overview
text:
Notes
From the text, the following three propositions are the important ones:

“A hands-on introduction to all of the essential tools you’ll need to build real, working websites.”
“You’ll learn what web developers actually do…”
“…the foundations you’ll need for later courses.”


Proposition 1: “A hands-on …”
A website can be categorized into the the following two:

Real-working ones
Bullshit ones

Let us focus on the real working ones. Consider the following real-working websites:

Netflix India – Watch TV Shows Online, Watch Movies Online
Airbnb | Holiday rentals, cabins, beach houses & more
Electric Cars, Solar & Clean Energy | Tesla

These are some real working websites full of life.
In order to build the above websites, some essential technologies are used. We don’t know what these are yet, but let us say the following:

ET 1
ET 2
ET 3
etc.

The Foundations Course gives us an introduction to all such essential technologies.
Now, the introduction could be of the following two flavours:

Theoretical
Practical

The Foundations Course gives us a hands-on practical introduction.
In the other courses we will be going deeper into each of these essential technologies so that one day we will be equipped with the tools to develop such websites ourselves!


Proposition 2: “You’ll …”
What web developers actually do? Will we know it at a deeper level? Like what goes inside their work? Day in a life kind of thing?






Thinking Beyond




What other professional people do?
What X actually do?
Should I have a general idea of them? How deep? Surface level?

Social setting: “What do you do?” I should know what most of the professions do? I can ask about the work. Perception or Reality? Fuck!!!
Anyways here is a list.




Proposition 3: “…the foundations…”
The Foundations Course prepares us for the next courses.

title: Colored Cups
text:
Introduction
I have had my share in teaching high school students a few years ago and one of the challenges that I faced back then was of getting a real time feedback of my student’s learning, as I was teaching in the class. Since I could not (and can not now as well) read their minds to know if they are following well along, I had to rely on them. They had to let me know if they are following along well. That brings another challenge! A student first must be self aware of his/her learning. In order to enable this, the students must be made owners of their learning.

title: Colored Cups
text:
Colored Cups
Colored Cups introduced by Dylan Wiliam in his book Embedded Formative Assessment (2011), is an effective technique that can be used in a classroom that attempts to:

Get real-time feedback of student’s learning for teachers.
Promotes self-regulated learning for students.

title: Colored Cups
text:
Working
The idea is simple!
All the students in the classrooms have 3 colored cups, a green cup, a yellow cup, and a red cup which are stacked together at the beginning of the class.
Each of these colors reflect the various levels of understanding. Green means the student is understanding well, yellow means s/he is not quite sure what is going on, and red means the student has no idea of what is going on.
As the lesson is being taught, the students put a colored cup on their desks that corresponds to how they are following along, and the teacher can see the classroom to get a sense of how the students are following along.
Prior to when a student puts a colored cup on the desk, s/he must self-assess his learning.

title: Colored Cups
text:
Adaptations In The fast.ai Course
I first got to know about this in Practical Deep Learning for Coders course. In the course, a virtual setup was made and the teacher, Jeremy could see it from his end in the teacher version.

title: Teaching AI Some Geography
text:
Lesson 1 of the Practical Deep Learning for Coders course offered by fast.ai gets hands-on on building an image classifier of your choice pretty quickly without any hastles from the get-go!
I tried to build an image classifier that could classify the 7 continents based on their map images. And boy let me tell you, I was unbelievably amazed by its results.
Let me show you how I did it! Feel free to try yourself on Colab or any platform of your choice!

title: Teaching AI Some Geography
text:
Setting Up

Install Packages
We begin by installing fastbook and duckduckgo_search. The formers allows us to access the fast.ai library that will help us build our image classifier and the latter allows us to access DuckDuckGo search engine features to search, get, and download our images for training.

!pip install -Uqq fastbook duckduckgo_search



Import Relevant Libraries
We import the relevant libraries. More about them here.

from duckduckgo_search import ddg_images
from fastcore.all import *
from fastdownload import download_url
from fastai.vision.all import *



Setup A System For Getting Images
Here, we create a handy little function that can search for a specific term on DuckDuckGo image searches and get its URLs

def search_images(term, max_images=30):
    print(f"Searching for '{term}'")
    return L(ddg_images(term, max_results=max_images)).itemgot('image')

For example, if our term is africa continent map, the above function gets the first popped up URL of the image of africa continent map as shown below.

urls = search_images('africa continent map', max_images=1)
urls[0]

Searching for 'africa continent map'


'https://cdn.onestopmap.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/05/464-map-africa-continent-political-shaded-relief.jpg'


Once, we get the URLs of an image, let us setup a way through which we can actually download the URLs we obtained and view them. We will storing the downloaded files in jpg formats.
The following snippets do that for us.

dest = 'africa.jpg'
download_url(urls[0], dest, show_progress=False)

im = Image.open(dest)
im.to_thumb(256,256)








The Africa continent looks good. Let us have sneak peak on the remaining 6 continents just to make sure that DuckDuckGo is getting the right images for us.

download_url(search_images('antarctica continent map', max_images=1)[0], 'antarctica.jpg', show_progress=False)
Image.open('antarctica.jpg').to_thumb(256,256)

Searching for 'antarctica continent map'










download_url(search_images('asia continent map', max_images=1)[0], 'asia.jpg', show_progress=False)
Image.open('asia.jpg').to_thumb(256,256)

Searching for 'asia continent map'










download_url(search_images('australia continent map', max_images=1)[0], 'australia.jpg', show_progress=False)
Image.open('australia.jpg').to_thumb(256,256)

Searching for 'australia continent map'










download_url(search_images('europe continent map', max_images=1)[0], 'europe.jpg', show_progress=False)
Image.open('europe.jpg').to_thumb(256,256)

Searching for 'europe continent map'










download_url(search_images('north america continent map', max_images=1)[0], 'north_america.jpg', show_progress=False)
Image.open('north_america.jpg').to_thumb(256,256)

Searching for 'north america continent map'










download_url(search_images('south america continent map', max_images=1)[0], 'south_america.jpg', show_progress=False)
Image.open('south_america.jpg').to_thumb(256,256)

Searching for 'south america continent map'









Everything, looks good!
Now that everything is setup, let us create our classifier. Generally, the following steps are taken when building an image classifier model.

title: Teaching AI Some Geography
text:
Get Image Data
Let us go on and get our image data.

searches = ['africa', 'antarctica', 'asia', 'australia', 'europe', 'north america', 'south america']
path = Path('which_continent_is_it')
from time import sleep

for o in searches:
    dest = (path/o)
    dest.mkdir(exist_ok=True, parents=True)
    download_images(dest, urls=search_images(f'{o} continent map'))
    sleep(10)  # Pause between searches to avoid over-loading server
    resize_images(path/o, max_size=400, dest=path/o)

Searching for 'africa continent map'
Searching for 'antarctica continent map'


/usr/local/lib/python3.7/dist-packages/PIL/Image.py:1015: UserWarning: Couldn't allocate palette entry for transparency
  warnings.warn("Couldn't allocate palette entry for transparency")


Searching for 'asia continent map'
Searching for 'australia continent map'


/usr/local/lib/python3.7/dist-packages/PIL/Image.py:960: UserWarning: Palette images with Transparency expressed in bytes should be converted to RGBA images
  "Palette images with Transparency expressed in bytes should be "


Searching for 'europe continent map'
Searching for 'north america continent map'
Searching for 'south america continent map'


/usr/local/lib/python3.7/dist-packages/PIL/Image.py:1015: UserWarning: Couldn't allocate palette entry for transparency
  warnings.warn("Couldn't allocate palette entry for transparency")


Sometimes, we might get a few broken images. We can track and discard them as follows.

failed = verify_images(get_image_files(path))
failed.map(Path.unlink)
len(failed)

7

title: Teaching AI Some Geography
text:
Create DataBlock
Next we create a DataBlock that helps us put our images into the model.

dls = DataBlock(
    blocks=(ImageBlock, CategoryBlock), 
    get_items=get_image_files, 
    splitter=RandomSplitter(valid_pct=0.2, seed=42),
    get_y=parent_label,
    item_tfms=[Resize(192, method='squish')]
).dataloaders(path, bs=32)

dls.show_batch(max_n=21)

title: Teaching AI Some Geography
text:
Create The Learner
Now, we create the actual model that will learn from the image data we gave it.

learn = vision_learner(dls, resnet18, metrics=error_rate)
learn.fine_tune(3)

/usr/local/lib/python3.7/dist-packages/torchvision/models/_utils.py:209: UserWarning: The parameter 'pretrained' is deprecated since 0.13 and will be removed in 0.15, please use 'weights' instead.
  f"The parameter '{pretrained_param}' is deprecated since 0.13 and will be removed in 0.15, "
/usr/local/lib/python3.7/dist-packages/torchvision/models/_utils.py:223: UserWarning: Arguments other than a weight enum or `None` for 'weights' are deprecated since 0.13 and will be removed in 0.15. The current behavior is equivalent to passing `weights=ResNet18_Weights.IMAGENET1K_V1`. You can also use `weights=ResNet18_Weights.DEFAULT` to get the most up-to-date weights.
  warnings.warn(msg)









epoch
train_loss
valid_loss
error_rate
time




0
2.544921
1.500386
0.538462
00:44












epoch
train_loss
valid_loss
error_rate
time




0
1.187040
0.556937
0.179487
01:04


1
0.718358
0.290570
0.089744
01:04


2
0.497790
0.240653
0.076923
01:02

title: Teaching AI Some Geography
text:
Test The Model
Our model is ready to be tested!
Let us input the images we stored at the beginning and see its results.

which_continent_is_it,_,probs = learn.predict(PILImage.create('africa.jpg'))
print(f"This is: {which_continent_is_it}.")
print(f"Probability: {probs[0]:.4f}")








This is: africa.
Probability: 0.9926



which_continent_is_it,_,probs = learn.predict(PILImage.create('antarctica.jpg'))
print(f"This is: {which_continent_is_it}.")
print(f"Probability: {probs[1]:.4f}")








This is: antarctica.
Probability: 0.9998



which_continent_is_it,_,probs = learn.predict(PILImage.create('asia.jpg'))
print(f"This is: {which_continent_is_it}.")
print(f"Probability: {probs[2]:.4f}")








This is: asia.
Probability: 1.0000



which_continent_is_it,_,probs = learn.predict(PILImage.create('australia.jpg'))
print(f"This is: {which_continent_is_it}.")
print(f"Probability: {probs[3]:.4f}")








This is: australia.
Probability: 0.9995



which_continent_is_it,_,probs = learn.predict(PILImage.create('europe.jpg'))
print(f"This is: {which_continent_is_it}.")
print(f"Probability: {probs[4]:.4f}")








This is: europe.
Probability: 0.9992



which_continent_is_it,_,probs = learn.predict(PILImage.create('north_america.jpg'))
print(f"This is: {which_continent_is_it}.")
print(f"Probability: {probs[5]:.4f}")








This is: north america.
Probability: 0.9999



which_continent_is_it,_,probs = learn.predict(PILImage.create('south_america.jpg'))
print(f"This is: {which_continent_is_it}.")
print(f"Probability: {probs[6]:.4f}")








This is: south america.
Probability: 0.9978


Looks pretty impressive isn’t it?!
If you have any ideas of your own feel free to try them out.
